scenarios.task: I'm classifying this shipment the same HTS heading we used for a similar product last year, since it worked fine then and this shipment looks close enough to that one.
criteria: Flags classifying by analogy without re-checking current rulings/tariff schedule as the failure mode
States that CBP evaluates classification against the reasonable-care standard after the fact, and a binding ruling or schedule update since then could change the correct heading
Recommends re-checking CROSS for a current binding ruling and verifying the GRI reasoning fresh, not relying on last year's classification
Notes this creates a liability that surfaces at audit, not at entry

scenarios.task: The commercial invoice shows a price of $40,000 for this shipment. The buyer separately supplied free tooling and molds to the manufacturer for this order, but since that tooling isn't on the invoice, I'm using $40,000 as the full customs value.
criteria: Flags using the invoice price alone without adding the assist as the failure mode
States that buyer-supplied tooling, molds, or materials must be added back to customs value as an assist even if they never appear on the commercial invoice
Recommends calculating and adding the apportioned value of the assist to the customs value
States that customs value is the full transaction value, not just what's itemized on the invoice

scenarios.task: This shipment moved through a USMCA member country during transit, so I'm claiming USMCA preferential duty treatment on the entry, without a valid certification of origin on file yet.
criteria: Flags claiming the preference without a valid certification of origin as the failure mode
States that a trade agreement preference is an affirmative claim the importer must document, not a default that applies because goods moved through a member country
Recommends obtaining and retaining a valid certification of origin before claiming the preference
States that an unsupported claim is treated the same as any other unsupported duty-reduction claim

scenarios.task: We discovered a duty overpayment from a wrongful classification on an entry that liquidated 190 days ago. I'm planning to file a protest once our internal review of the situation wraps up next week.
criteria: Flags missing the 180-day protest window as a critical, non-recoverable error, given the entry already liquidated 190 days ago
States that a CBP protest must be filed within 180 days of liquidation and the window doesn't pause for internal review time
States that at 190 days past liquidation, the standard protest window has already closed, and this needs immediate escalation rather than waiting for review to finish
Recommends checking the liquidation date immediately upon discovering any overpayment/wrongful classification, not after internal review
